content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 2, 1993

Honorable Joel Keller
Mayor, City of Antioch
P.O. Box 130
Antioch, CA 94509

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-93-222

Dear Mayor Keller:

This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/
Since you have not described a specific pending governmental
decision, your advice is being treated as a request for informal
assistance.2/

QUESTION

If a member of the city council is disqualified from
participating in a decision, may the councilmember represent his
or her own personal interests as member of the public before the
council?

CONCLUSION

Commission Regulation 18700.1(b) permits public officials to
represent their own "personal interests" before their agency in
the same manner as any other member of the general public.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-93-222
Page Two


DISCUSSION

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in, or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a financial interest.

A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence, include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1(a).)

However, should a public official determine that a conflict of interest exists and is disqualified from participating in a decision, Commission Regulation 18700.1(b)(1) (copy enclosed) allows a public official to comment, as a member of the public, at public meetings, forums, or hearings on issues related solely to the effect of a decision on his or her "personal interests."

A "personal interest" includes an interest in real property which is wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family, a business entity wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family, and a business entity over which the official exercises sole direction and control, or over which the official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction and control. (Regulation 18700.1(b)(1).) A councilmember may not testify on behalf of any other person or group.

Accordingly, once a city councilmember has been formally disqualified from participating and has announced the reason therefor on the public record (Regulation 18700(b)(5)), the councilmember may address the council under the same procedures applicable to receipt of comments from members of the public.

I trust this has provided the guidance you need for future reference. If you have any other questions, please feel free to contact the Commission's Legal Division at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

JM/JET/jt
Enclosure
